coredata.dc:title: Photothermal effect enhancing graphene quantum dots/semiconducting polymer/nanozyme-mediated cancer catalytic therapy
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Linyu
Sun, Weiyi
Tang, Yuying
Li, Su
Zhang, Bei
Sun, Xuetong
Ji, Wenwen
Ma, Lijuan
Deng, Haishan
Han, Shuying
Zhu, Dong
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with intrinsic enzyme-like activity have attracted great attention in the biomedical field. The application mainly focused on biosensing area, but the anti-tumor study was restricted due to the poor catalytic efficacy of GQDs. Herein, we introduce an enhancing nanozyme using graphene quantum dots/semiconducting polymer nanocomposites (GQD-SPNs) which provide enhanced nanozyme activity for tumor therapy through photothermal effect. Near infrared irradiation guide local temperature increasing at the tumor and boost the hydroxyl radicals (▪OH) generation, to synergize with generated 1O2 and heat for better cancer therapy in Hela tumor bearing-mice. The present GQD-SPNs may turn on great opportunities for the enhanced nanozymes to cancer catalytic therapy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Nanozyme
Cancer catalytic therapy
Semiconducting polymer
Photothermal effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In vitro anti-tumor activity and ROS regulation of GQD-SPNs in tumor cells
Photothermal effect enhancing cancer catalytic therapy in Hela tumor-bearing mice

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic demonstration of the preparation of GQD-SPNs; (b) and underlying synergism of photothermal effect enhancing GQD-SPNs/nanozyme-mediated cancer catalytic therapy. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) TEM image of the GQDs; (b) FT-IR spectra of the GQDs; (c) Influences of temperature on the POD-like property of the GQDs; (d) Influences of pH on the POD-like property of the GQDs; (e) Michaelis-Menten analysis of the POD-like property of the GQDs; (f) Left: TMB(1 mM), H2O2 (25 mM), colorless; Right: TMB (1 mM), H2O2 (25 mM), GQD (20 μg mL−1), dark blue. The reaction was carried out in 400 μL PBS Buffer (pH = 4, T = 45 °C). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) TEM image of the SPNs (scale bars = 50 nm); (b) TEM image of the GQD-SPNs; (c) DLS profiles of the SPNs and the GQD-SPNs (dispersion solvent: deionized H2O); (d) Zeta potentials of the GQDs, the SPNs and the GQD-SPNs (dispersion solvent: deionized H2O); (e) UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of the SPNs and the GQD-SPNs; (f) Photothermal curves of PBS, the SPNs and the GQD-SPNs (power density = 1W·cm−2). Inserts were the thermal images of PBS, the SPNs and the GQD-SPNs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Relative cell viability of Hela cells incubated with SPNs or GQD-SPNs at different concentrations for 24 h or 48 h (a) without or (b) with NIR laser irradiation at 808 nm (1 W cm−2) for 5 min (∗p < 0.05, ∗∗p < 0.01, ∗∗∗p < 0.001, n = 3); (c) Fluorescence images of Hela cells incubated with PBS, GQDs, SPNs or GQD-SPNs with or without NIR laser irradiation. (scale bars = 200 μm); (d) Intracellular DCF fluorescence intensity measured by multimode microplate reader. The cells were stained by H2DCFDA for ROS detection. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) In vivo NIR fluorescence images of Hela tumor-bearing mice at different time points after tail vein injection of SPNs or GQD-SPNs. The tumors were denoted with the red dashed circles. The images were captured with excitation and emission at 745 nm and 810–875 nm, respectively; (b) In vivo Fluorescence intensities of tumors at various time points (n = 3); (c) Ex vivo NIR fluorescence images of main organs and tumors at 24 h after injection of SPNs or GQD-SPNs. The quantitative analysis was performed on Living Image 4.2. (d) Ex vivo Fluorescence intensities of main organs and tumors at 24 h after injection of nanomaterials (n = 3). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) IR thermal images of Hela tumor-bearing mice under NIR laser irradiation (808 nm, 1W·cm−2) at 24 h after vein injection of saline, SPNs or GQD-SPNs. The tumors were denoted with the white dashed circles; (b) Temperature curves of mice at 24 h after administration of saline, SPNs or GQD-SPNs; (c) Body weight data of mice after administration with saline, SPNs, GQD-SPNs with or without laser irradiation; (d) Tumor growth curves of mice after treatment with saline, SPNs, GQD-SPNs with or without laser irradiation (∗∗p < 0.01, ∗∗∗p < 0.001, n = 3); (e) Digital photographs of tumors at day 16 with different treatments; (f) Histological H&E staining of heart, liver, spleen, lung and kidney at day 16 after treatment with saline, SPNs, GQD-SPNs with or without irradiation (scale bars = 100 μm); (g) Histological H&E staining of tumor at day 16 after treatment with saline, SPNs, GQD-SPNs with or without irradiation (scale bars = 100 μm). The necrotic tumor areas were indicated with the black dashed circles. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Comparison of Michaelis constant (Km) and maximum velocity (Vmax) between GQDs and other reported POD-like nanozymes.

head.ce:title: Photothermal effect enhancing graphene quantum dots/semiconducting polymer/nanozyme-mediated cancer catalytic therapy

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Nanjing University of Chinese Medicine, Nanjing, Jiangsu, 210023, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Nanjing University of Chinese Medicine, Nanjing, Jiangsu, 210023, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with intrinsic enzyme-like activity have attracted great attention in the biomedical field. The application mainly focused on biosensing area, but the anti-tumor study was restricted due to the poor catalytic efficacy of GQDs. Herein, we introduce an enhancing nanozyme using graphene quantum dots/semiconducting polymer nanocomposites (GQD-SPNs) which provide enhanced nanozyme activity for tumor therapy through photothermal effect. Near infrared irradiation guide local temperature increasing at the tumor and boost the hydroxyl radicals (▪OH) generation, to synergize with generated 1O2 and heat for better cancer therapy in Hela tumor bearing-mice. The present GQD-SPNs may turn on great opportunities for the enhanced nanozymes to cancer catalytic therapy.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, various nanomaterials have been demonstrated as “nanozymes” that hold enzyme-like activity, such as cerium oxide, iron oxide, vanadium oxide, graphene oxide and so on [1–6]. These nanozymes comply with the enzymatic principle of natural enzymes, and can effectively perform some enzymatic reactions under normal physiological conditions. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are a type of zero-dimensional nanomaterial composed of small graphene fragments [7]. In recent years, GQDs have arisen great interest in the fields of biomedical due to their peroxidase (POD)-like activity and good biocompatibility as a nanozyme. Owing to the generation of hydroxyl radicals (▪OH) during the POD-like catalytic reaction, GQDs have been exploited in the biosensing and antibacterial study, such as glucose detection, H2O2 detection and wound disinfection [8–10]. However, the anti-tumor research was unfortunately restricted due to the insufficient generation of ▪OH as a tumor cell killer, which is limited by the low catalytic efficiency of GQDs. Similar to natural enzymes, temperature is a great influential factor in regulating nanozyme activity [2,11]. Thus, operable temperature control makes it possible to regulate POD-like activity of GQDs in living systems.

In this study, we report a nanozyme, GQD-SPNs, which can enhance the POD-like activity of GQDs through a photothermal process for better tumor catalytic therapy. Such the nanocomposite is composed of GQDs and SPNs doped with near-infrared dye IR780 [12], serving as temperature-sensitive nanozyme and photothermal conversion agent, respectively. Under NIR laser irradiation, the GQD-SPNs not only generate plenty of singlet oxygen (1O2), but also can efficiently transfer the light energy into heat, causing local temperature rise in tumor sites and enhancing the POD-like activity of GQDs (Fig. 1 (b)). The GQD-SPNs with enhanced enzyme activity can catalyze the endogenous H2O2 to generate much more hydroxyl radicals (▪OH), to synergize with generated 1O2 and heat for better cancer therapy.

MEH-PPV and IR780 were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. MWCNTs and 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent. PEI (Mw = 10,000) was purchased from Rhawn Reagent (Shanghai, China). 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescin diacetate (H2DCFDA) was purchased from Beyotime Biotechnology (China). The human cervical cancer cell line (HeLa) was purchased from the Cell Bank of Type Culture Collection of Chinese Academy of Sciences. 4–5 weeks old female BALB/c nude mice were purchased from Shanghai SLAC laboratory Animal Co., Ltd (China).

TEM images were obtained on a JEM-1200 EX with an accelerating voltage of 100 kV. DLS and zeta potential were obtained from the Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90. UV–vis and fluorescence spectra were recorded by the Tecan SPARK 10 M multimode microplate reader. FT-IR spectra was obtained on a Thermo Nicolet IS5 FT-IR spectrometer. IR thermal images were recorded by a photothermal camera Optris PI450. Cell imaging was observed by Leica DMI8 Inverted Fluorescent Microscope. In vivo fluorescence images were recorded on the IVIS Lumina XR system.

The GQDs with abundant specific oxygenated groups were synthesized according to the report of Qu’s Group [13]. Briefly, 50 mg of MWCNTs and 150 mL of concentrated HNO3 were added into a round-bottomed flask, refluxing at 135 °C for 72 h. The mixture color changed from deep black to pale yellow. Then NaOH was used to regulate the pH to 8 under the ice bath. The solution was then dialyzed in a 1000 Da dialysis bag for 3 days. The powder of GQDs were finally obtained by freeze-drying.

SPNs were prepared by a nano-coprecipitation method. Briefly, MEH-PPV, IR780 and PEI were individually dissolved in THF, CH3OH and CH3OH to make 1 mg mL−1 stock solutions. Subsequently, MEH-PPV (0.25 mg), IR780 (0.025 mg) and PEI (0.30 mg) were added into 4 mL THF and sonicated for 20 min. Then 10 mL H2O was injected quickly into the mixture system followed by sonicating for 20 min. After the formation of SPNs, THF and CH3OH were removed under nitrogen atmosphere at 60 °C. The SPNs were then purified and concentrated by centrifugation (3000 rpm, 30 min) for further use.

GQDs were conjuncted to the surface of SPNs through electrostatic interaction. In brief, the GQD-SPNs were prepared by directly incubating SPNs (1 mg) with GQDs (0.2 mg) in deionized H2O (1 mL) in the dark. The GQD-SPNs were further purified by ultrafiltration to remove unbound GQDs. (Mw = 30 KD).

The peroxidase (POD)-like activity of GQDs was measured in 25 mM PBS Buffer with TMB as substrate and H2O2 as oxidation substrate. The color reaction was recorded on the Tecan SPARK 10 M multimode microplate reader at 652 nm. The pH dependence of the POD-like activity of GQDs was measured in the PBS buffer solutions at different pH values from 2 to 8, and the temperature dependence was measured in buffer solutions at different temperatures from 20 °C to 60 °C. The kinetic assays of GQDs with TMB as substrate was performed by adding different concentrations of TMB into PBS buffer containing 20 μg mL−1 GQDs and 25 mM H2O2. The reactions were monitored by microplate reader at different reaction times, and the Michaelis–Menten constant was calculated according to the Lineweaver-Burk equation: 1/v = (Km/Vmax)/C + 1/Vmax.

PBS, SPNs and GQD-SPNs with a concentration of 250 μg mL−1 were irradiated with an 808 nm Laser for 5 min (power density = 1 W cm−2). The temperature changes were captured by a photothermal camera (Optris PI450) every 0.5 min.
Hela cells were cultured in Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM) with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1% penicillin-streptomycin solution at 37 °C under 5% CO2.

Hela cells were seeded in 96-well plates with the concentration of 8000 cells per well. After a 12 h cultivation, different concentrations of SPNs or GQD-SPNs were incubated with the cells for 12 h. The cells were then processed with or without laser irradiation. After incubating for another 12 h or 36 h, fresh medium containing 0.5 mg mL−1 MTT was added to each well. After incubating for another 4 h, the medium was removed and 150 μL DMSO was added to each well to dissolve the formazans. The absorbance of each well was measured on the SPARK 10 M microplate reader at 490 nm.

Hela cells were seeded in confocal dishes with the concentration of 4 × 104 cells per dish. After a 12 h cultivation, fresh medium containing 100 μg mL−1 SPNs or GQD-SPNs were added to replace the initial medium. After incubation for 6 h in the dark, cells were washed three times with PBS, and fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde for 15 min. The fluorescence and bright field images were subsequently captured by a fluorescence microscope (Ex:550 nm, Em: 620–750 nm). The merged images were obtained by Image J software.

The intracellular ROS level was monitored by fluorescence microscope using H2DCFDA as a fluorescent probe. Briefly, Hela cells were seeded in confocal dishes. After a 24 h cultivation, fresh medium containing 20 μg mL−1 GQDs and 100 μg mL−1 SPNs or GQD-SPNs were added to replace the initial medium. After incubation for 6 h in the dark, cells were washed three times with PBS, and 10 μM H2DCFDA were added and incubated for 15 min. After that, the cells were rinsed again with PBS and irradiated with an 808 nm laser (1W·cm−2) for 5 min. The fluorescence images were subsequently captured by a fluorescence microscope to compare the ROS production. (Ex: 488 nm, Em: 525 nm). And the quantitative data were obtained by the microplate reader.

All animal experiments were conducted under the rules established by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee (IACUC), Sing Health. To establish the xenografted tumor models, 2.5 × 106 Hela cells suspended in 0.1 mL PBS were subcutaneous injected into the back of mice. Hela tumor-bearing mice were used for further near infrared fluorescence (NIRF) imaging, cancer therapy and histological analysis when the tumor volume reached about 100 mm3.

In vivo NIRF images were captured from the IVIS Lumina XR system with excitation and emission at 745 nm and 810–875 nm, respectively. Firstly, NIRF images at 0 h were captured before injection of nanoparticles. Hela tumor-bearing mice were then injected with SPNs or GQD-SPNs (200 μL, 250 μg mL−1) via the tail vein. And the NIRF images were then collected at various time points (4, 6, 8, 10 and 24 h). At 24 h post-injection, mice in each group were dissected, and the main organs and tumor were harvested for ex vivo imaging. All image data were finally analyzed on a Living Image 4.2 software.

When the tumor reached about 100 mm3, 25 Hela tumor-bearing mice were stochastically divided into 5 groups. The groups were set as follows: Group 1: PBS; Group 2: SPNs; Group 3: SPNs + Laser; Group 4: GQD-SPNs; Group 5: GQD-SPNs + Laser. For treatment, mice were injected with PBS, SPNs or GQD-SPNs (200 μL, 250 μg mL−1) via the tail vein. At 24 h post-injection, the mice in Group 3 and 5 were irradiated with an 808 nm Laser for 5 min (1W·cm−2) and a photothermal camera was used to capture the temperature change of the tumor site every 0.5 min. To monitor the therapeutic effect and biosafety, the tumor volume (V = width2 × length/2) and body weight of mice were measured every two days.

After 16 days of treatment, the organs and tumor of mice were harvested and fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde for Histological Analysis. The fixed tissue was embedded in paraffin and sectioned for further H&E staining. And the section images were later captured by a microscope.

The preparation of GQD-SPNs consisted of three steps (Fig. 1(a)): synthesis of GQDs according to the previous report [13], preparation of SPNs and conjunction between GQDs and SPNs. The prepared GQDs had a uniform particle size of 5.2 nm (Fig. 2 (a)), and the surface was rich in oxygenated groups from the corresponding FT-IR spectra (Fig. 2(b)), the bands around 1617 cm−1 and 3439 cm−1 were respectively assigned to the stretching vibration of CO and O–H in carboxyl groups, which were considered as the essential functional groups to exert the POD-like activity [14]. The absorption and fluorescence spectrum of GQDs was shown in Fig. S1. The synthesized GQDs showed peroxidase-mimicking capacity and enabled catalyze the colorless 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to produce a blue solution, indicating generation of hydroxyl radicals (Fig. 2(f)). As indicated in Fig. 2(c), (d), the catalytic efficiency of GQDs nanozyme depended on temperature and pH, which were optimized to T = 45 °C and pH = 4, respectively. Furthermore, the Michaelis constant (Km) and the maximum velocity (Vmax) were determined to be 4.175 × 10−2 mM and 13.55 × 10−8 Ms−1 according to Michaelis-Menten model (Fig. 2(e)). In the second step, SPNs were prepared from branched PEI (Mw = 10, 000), MEH-PPV and near-infrared dye IR780 through a nano-precipitation process. MEH-PPV is a conjugated semiconducting polymer with good biocompatibility, photostability and easy modification. IR780 is a lipophilic heptamethine dye with excellent fluorescent, photothermal and photodynamic conversion characteristics [15,16]. The percentage of IR780 in SPNs was 1.61% according to the standard curve (Fig. S2). Subsequently, the above-prepared GQDs were attached to SPNs through electrostatic attraction between the negatively charged groups on the GQDs and the positively charged groups on the PEI.

After electrostatic bonding, the diameter of the GQD-SPNs nanozyme increased from 25 nm for SPNs to 35 nm for GQD-SPNs (Fig. 3 (a), (b)), confirmed by the dynamic light scattering (DLS) assay (Fig. 3(c)). The zeta potential of GQDs and SPNs was −38.9 mV and 42.3 mV, respectively. The corresponding zeta potential of the GQD-SPNs was changed to −23.1 mV (Fig. 3(d)), indicating the successful bonding between GQDs and SPNs. The FT-IR spectrum of GQD-SPNs also proved the successful connection between GQDs and SPNs (Fig. S3), the bands (Fig. S3(b)) around 3435 cm−1 and 1625 cm−1 were respectively assigned to the stretching vibration and bending vibration of N–H in PEI [17], after combined with GQDs on the surface (Fig. S3(c)), the characteristic bands around 3438 cm−1, 1618 cm−1, 1384 cm−1 of GQDs appeared. The Michaelis-Menten analysis of the POD-like property of GQDs and GQD-SPNs indicated the connection between GQDs and SPNs have no significant effect on the POD-like activity of GQDs (Fig. S4).

As shown in Fig. 3(e), the GQD-SPNs exhibited an almost same absorption and fluorescence spectrum compared with SPNs. The characteristic absorption peaks at 500 nm and 800 nm belonged to MEH-PPV and IR780, as well as the emission peaks at 596 nm and 826 nm belonged to MEH-PPV and IR780, respectively. Afterward, the photothermal properties of SPNs and GQD-SPNs were monitored by a photothermal camera (Optris PI450) every 0.5 min. When the solutions were irradiated with 808 nm for 5 min, the temperature of both SPNs and GQD-SPNs rose up to about 47 °C, while the temperature of PBS did not change significantly (Fig. 3(f)).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro anti-tumor activity and ROS regulation of GQD-SPNs in tumor cells
ce:section.ce:para: The enhanced nanozyme catalytic effect and in vitro anti-tumor activity of GQD-SPNs with photothermal properties was investigated in Hela cells. As indicated in Fig. 4 (a), without NIR laser irradiation, the SPNs had neglectable cytotoxicity to Hela cells due to none of photothermal conversion, while the GQD-SPNs had a little more cytotoxicity than SPNs due to the weak peroxidase-like activity of GQDs at normal temperature. However, when irradiated with NIR laser, the GQD-SPNs presented much higher cytotoxicity to Hela cells than SPNs. When the concentration of nanocomposites was up to 250 μg mL−1, the viability of Hela cells incubated with GQD-SPNs for 24 h (20.54%) was significantly decreased by 2.46-fold as compared with SPNs (50.47%), when incubated for 48 h, the viability of GQD-SPNs treated cells dramatically reduced to 9.18%, while the SPNs treated group was 38.47% (Fig. 4(b)). The significantly enhanced anti-tumor effect of GQD-SPNs under NIR laser irradiation was ascribed to the temperature rise caused by photothermal effect as well as the greatly improved ROS level from IR780 and GQDs (Fig. 1(b)).

Before the ROS assay, the cellular uptake capacity of nanoparticles was investigated. As shown in Fig. S6, the strong fluorescence signal was observed in the cells after incubation with SPNs or GQD-SPNs (C = 100 μg mL−1) for 6 h, indicating the nanoparticles can be endocytose into cells efficiently. The intracellular ROS level was further assayed by a fluorescent probe, H2DCFDA, which can emit green fluorescence after being oxidized by intracellular ROS [18]. As shown in Fig. 4(c), (d), cells treated with GQDs or GQD-SPNs without NIR laser irradiation exhibited weak fluorescence signal (～0.50 × 104 a. u.) due to the weak catalytic efficiency of GQDs at normal temperature. However, under NIR laser irradiation, the fluorescence signal of Hela cells treated with GQD-SPNs (3.48 × 104 a. u.) was increased by 1.96-fold as compared with that of SPNs (1.78 × 104 a. u.), indicating GQD-SPNs could induce much more ROS production than SPNs, leading to much more toxicity to tumor cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal effect enhancing cancer catalytic therapy in Hela tumor-bearing mice
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the bio-distribution and metabolism of nanoparticles in vivo, the GQD-SPNs or SPNs (200 μL, 250 μg mL−1) solution were injected into Hela tumor bearing-mice via tail vein and the fluorescence images were recorded and analyzed by IVIS Living Image. As indicated in Fig. 5 (a), (b) and Fig. S7, the fluorescence intensities of GQD-SPNs and SPNs treated mice gradually increased and reached their highest values at 24 h. The main organs were harvested for fluorescent imaging at 24 h post-injection. As indicated in Fig. 5(c), (d) and Fig. S8, both GQD-SPNs and SPNs tended to concentrate in tumor and organs with plentiful blood supply, such as liver. The fluorescence intensities of the GQD-SPNs nanocomposites in tumor were 8.22, 1.64, 4.24, 7.03 and 4.93 times higher than that in the heart, liver, spleen, lung and kidney, respectively. All of these verified the accumulation of GQD-SPNs and SPNs in tumor site due to the strong permeability and retention (EPR) effect.

As shown in Fig. 6 (a), (b), after 24 h intravenous injection of the GQD-SPNs and SPNs into the tumor-bearing mice, both of the tumor temperatures increased rapidly and reached maximums to about 45 °C with NIR laser irradiation, which was the just optimal temperature for GQDs to provide highest enzyme activity. The tumor volumes and body weights of mice were recorded every two days for 16 days. The GQD-SPNs completely inhibited the tumor growth for 16 days under NIR irradiation, while the corresponding SPNs failed (Fig. 6(d), (e)), which was ascribed to the combination of photothermal, photodynamic and enhanced nanozyme-medicated catalytic therapy in GQD-SPNs group, while the SPNs only generated photothermal and photodynamic effect. Under NIR irradiation, the GQD-SPNs enable not only generate plenty of singlet oxygen (1O2) in Fig. 4 c, d, but also can efficiently transfer the light energy into heat, causing local temperature rise in tumor sites (Fig. 6(a)) and enhancing enzyme activity to generate much more hydroxyl radicals (▪OH) (
Fig. 2
f), to synergize with generated 1O2 and heat for better cancer therapy. Besides, the tumor growth rate of SPNs-injected mice without NIR irradiation was similar to the saline group, which indicate the non-toxicity of the SPNs without laser irradiation. Moreover, there was no significant changes in weight of each group, indicating the good biological safety of all nanoparticles (Fig. 6(c)).

To further verify the biosafety and therapeutic efficiency by using the GQD-SPNs, the main organs and tumor of mice in each group were harvested for H&E staining. As shown in Fig. 6(f), no obvious histopathological lesions were observed in the organs of all groups, indicating the good biosafety of all nanoparticles. In contrast, the tumor of the mice treated with the nanoparticles appeared different extents of cell necrosis (Fig. 6(g)). The necrotic area of GQD-SPNs + Laser group reached up to 95%, 2.11-fold and 6.79-fold higher than SPNs + Laser group (45%) and GQD-SPNs - Laser group (14%), respectively. The results further proved the super anti-tumor effect of GQD-SPNs + Laser, which was contributed to the combined effects of the SPNs and GQDs under laser irradiation.

Compared with the natural enzyme and recently reported nanozymes with POD-like activity, the synthesized GQDs exhibited much lower Km and higher Vmax values than those listed in Table 1 . These results indicated the excellent catalytic activity of the GQDs under the optimal conditions.

The enhanced nanozymes properties of the GQD-SPNs enable them to be used as effective cancer catalytic therapeutic agent, which will enhance nanozyme activity for tumor therapy through photothermal effect. The advantages of the GQD-SPNs in multifunctional synergy, high effectiveness and good biocompatibility will make them competent for cancer catalytic therapy, compared to other nanozymes with low catalytic efficiency, single and poor therapeutic effect. Taken together, this study provides new ideas for the combination of photothermal and nanozyme-mediated catalytic therapy, we believe the strategy of the nanocomposite enzyme will have great potential for catalytic therapy in future.

Linyu Hu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Writing - original draftWriting – original draft, preparation. Weiyi Sun: Validation, Methodology, Visualization, Investigation. Yuying Tang: Formal analysis, Resources. Su Li: Software, Validation. Bei Zhang: Data curation. Xuetong Sun: Formal analysis. Wenwen Ji: Software, Validation. Lijuan Ma: Software, Validation. Haishan Deng: Visualization, Investigation. Shuying Han: Supervision, Writing - review & editingWriting – review & editing. Dong Zhu: Project administration, Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editingWriting – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (81573388). We also thank the financial support by the “Six Talent Peaks Project of Jiangsu Province (YY-032)” and the Open Project of Chinese Materia Medica First-Class Discipline of Nanjing University of Chinese Medicine (No. 2020YLXK025).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cerium oxide nanoparticles: applications and prospects in nanomedicine

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Das, J. M. Dowding, K. E. Klump, J. F. McGinnis, W. Self, S. Seal, Cerium oxide nanoparticles: applications and prospects in nanomedicine, Nanomedicine 8 (9) (2013) 1483-1508.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Intrinsic peroxidase-like activity of ferromagnetic nanoparticles

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Gao, J. Zhuang, L. Nie, J. Zhang, Y. Zhang, N. Gu, T. Wang, J. Feng, D. Yang, S. Perrett, X. Yan, Intrinsic peroxidase-like activity of ferromagnetic nanoparticles, Nat. Nanotechnol. 2 (9) (2007) 577-583.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Copper-Based metal–organic framework nanoparticles with peroxidase-like activity for sensitive colorimetric detection of Staphylococcus aureus

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Wang, W. Deng, L. Yang, Y. Tan, Q. Xie, S. Yao, Copper-Based Metal-Organic Framework Nanoparticles with Peroxidase-Like Activity for Sensitive Colorimetric Detection of Staphylococcus aureus, ACS Applied Materials & Interfaces 9 (29) (2017) 24440-24445.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphene oxide: intrinsic peroxidase catalytic activity and its application to glucose detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Song, K. Qu, C. Zhao, J. Ren, X. Qu, Graphene Oxide: Intrinsic Peroxidase Catalytic Activity and Its Application to Glucose Detection, Advanced Materials 22 (19) (2010) 2206-2210.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: V2O5 nanowires with an intrinsic peroxidase-like activity

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Andre, F. Natalio, M. Humanes, J. Leppin, K. Heinze, R. Wever, H. Schroder, W. Muller, W. Tremel, V2O5 Nanowires with an Intrinsic Peroxidase-Like Activity, Advanced Functional Materials 21 (3) (2011) 501-509.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Size-controlled preparation of peroxidase-like graphene-gold nanoparticle hybrids for the visible detection of norovirus-like particles

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. R. Ahmed, K. Takemeura, T. C. Li, N. Kitamoto, T. Tanaka, T. Suzuki, E. Y. Park, Size-controlled preparation of peroxidase-like graphene-gold nanoparticle hybrids for the visible detection of norovirus-like particles, Biosens Bioelectron 87 (2017) 558-565.
